content.problemStatement:
The structure of the company ShareChat can be described by a simple graph (without multiedges and self-loops) with NNN vertices numbered 111 through NNN and MMM edges numbered 111 through MMM. Each vertex of this graph is part of at most one simple cycle.

For each edge, you should find the number of simple paths that contain this edge and only contain at most one edge which belongs to a cycle.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N and M. M lines follow. For each valid i, the i-th of these lines contains two space-separated integers u and v, denoting the vertices connected by edge i.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print M lines. For each i (1≤i≤M), the i-th of these lines should contain a single integer — the number of paths for edge i.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first test case has 7 nodes and 7 edges. The graph has two cycles: 2-3-4 and 2-5-3. The number of paths that contain each edge and only contain at most one edge which belongs to a cycle is calculated and printed. The second test case has 3 nodes and 3 edges, and it has one cycle: 1-2-3. The number of paths that contain each edge and only contain at most one edge which belongs to a cycle is calculated and printed.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a graph traversal algorithm such as Depth-First Search (DFS) to detect cycles and count the number of paths that contain each edge. We will also use a technique called 'low-link' values to keep track of the smallest index reachable from each node, which helps us to detect cycles.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T*(N+M)) — because we are performing DFS for each test case, and in the worst case, we might need to visit all nodes and edges.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N+M) — because we need to store the graph and the low-link values for each node.
